1: 'Your eyes behind your veil are doves' — the *wasf* (descriptive praise poem) of the bridegroom begins. The veil (*tzamah*, covering) was worn by women for honor and modesty in ancient Israelite society; the eyes visible through or above the veil heightened their perceived beauty. 'Hair like a flock of goats cascading down Gilead' — the movement of a flock of goats descending a hillside in the Gilead region (the Transjordanian highlands) was a familiar pastoral sight: flowing, dark, and in motion. The comparison describes the beloved's hair as simultaneously numerous, dark, and graceful.
2: 'Your teeth are like a flock of freshly shorn, washed sheep, each with its twin — not one missing' — the dental description is among the most unusual but reflects ANE standards: white, matched teeth (none missing) were a mark of health and beauty. The freshly washed sheep are brilliantly white; 'each with its twin' (each tooth with its opposite) = a full, matching set. The ANE world was without modern dentistry; a full set of matched white teeth was genuinely remarkable.
3: 'Your lips are like a scarlet thread, your mouth lovely; your cheeks like pomegranate halves behind your veil' — scarlet thread (*chut hashani*) was used to mark Rahab's window (Joshua 2:18) and as part of the tabernacle furnishings (Exodus 25:4); here it marks the beloved's lips with the same distinctive color. The pomegranate (*rimmon*) half's blush of rose-red through the white veil describes the rosy cheeks. Pomegranates appeared on the hem of the high priest's robe (Exodus 28:33-34) and on the tops of the temple pillars (1 Kings 7:20).
4: 'Your neck is like the tower of David, built in courses of stone, a thousand shields hanging on it — all warriors' shields' — the tower of David (*migdal David*) was a known Jerusalem fortification, the kind of tower on which shields and weapons were displayed (Ezekiel 27:11 describes shields and helmets hung on Tyre's towers). The beloved's neck, adorned with multiple strands of jewelry, is compared to such a tower — strong, adorned, and marking military prestige. The thousand shields = the multiple rows of necklace strands that cover the neck like a tower's decorative armory.
5: 'Your two breasts are like two young fawns, twins of a gazelle, feeding among the lilies' — the twin fawns (*tefaché tze'vi*, young gazelles) are paired, delicate, and gentle in their movement. The gazelle fawn was a standard of tenderness and grace in ANE aesthetics. 'Feeding among the lilies' returns to the pastoral setting that recurs throughout the Song — beauty and gentleness located in the most abundant and fragrant natural setting.
6: 'Until the day breaks and shadows flee, I will go to the mountain of myrrh and the hill of frankincense' — myrrh (*mor*) and frankincense (*levonah*) were the two primary aromatic resins of the ancient sacrificial and cultic world; frankincense was the incense ingredient of the tabernacle (Exodus 30:34) and appears in the NT as one of the magi's gifts (Matthew 2:11). The beloved describes his beloved's body with the language of the most sacred aromatic substances — her presence is like the fragrance of the sanctuary.
7: 'You are altogether beautiful, my love; there is no defect in you' — the word *mum* (blemish, defect) is the technical cultic term for the disqualifying flaw in a sacrificial animal: only animals *without blemish* (*tamim*) were acceptable (Leviticus 22:21; Numbers 6:14). The bridegroom describes the beloved using the language of sacrificial acceptability: she is not only beautiful but unblemished in the technical, cultic sense — the kind of perfection that makes her acceptable as an offering. The NT applies this standard to Christ (Hebrews 9:14: who through the eternal Spirit offered himself without blemish to God).
8: 'Come with me from Lebanon, from the peak of Amana, from the summit of Senir and Hermon' — Amana (*Aamanah*) is the mountain range north of Damascus; Senir and Hermon were Amorite and Aramean names respectively for the same or adjacent peaks of the Anti-Lebanon range. These were the northern frontier mountains of Israel's sphere — beyond which lay the dangerous wilderness and the territory of foreign powers. The 'dens of lions and mountain haunts of leopards' were literal: large predators inhabited these highlands in antiquity. The summons is to come from danger to safety, from the wild frontier to the beloved's presence.
9: 'You have stolen my heart with one glance, with one jewel of your necklace' — the verb *libbavtini* (you have hearted me) is a denominative construction from *lev* (heart): your single glance has taken the heart out of him. In the honor-shame culture of the ancient near east, the power of a woman's glance was recognized and discussed — 'the evil eye' (a harmful glance) and the 'beautiful eye' had real social power. The 'one jewel' of the necklace = the power of a single element of the beloved's beauty to overwhelm.
10: 'How much better is your love than wine' — the comparison echoes and elevates Song of Solomon 1:2 (where the woman said the beloved's love was better than wine); now the man says the same of her love. The reciprocity of the comparison marks the mutuality of the Song: both voices use the same terms, value the same things, desire the same person. Perfumes better than 'any spice' — the word for spice (*bosem*) covers a wide range of aromatic substances; no specific spice could match the beloved's fragrance.
11: 'Your lips drip with nectar; honey and milk are under your tongue; the fragrance of your garments is like Lebanon' — honey (*devash*) and milk (*chalav*) together are the standard description of the Promised Land (Exodus 3:8; Song of Solomon 33:3; Numbers 13:27; Deuteronomy 6:3) — the land's abundance expressed through its two most valued natural products. The beloved's speech and presence evoke the abundance of the Promised Land itself; she is, in the beloved's experience, what the land promised to Israel. The fragrance of Lebanon (*levanon*) = the cedars' resinous scent, the most powerful natural aromatic of the region.
12: 'My sister, my bride, is a locked garden, a sealed spring, a closed fountain' — the locked garden (*gan na'ul*) is among the most explicit ANE symbols for female virginity and exclusivity. The Egyptian wisdom text Insinger Papyrus uses garden/spring imagery for female virtue; Mesopotamian texts use the sealed/locked vessel metaphor. The three images (locked garden, sealed spring, closed fountain) repeat the same idea with increasing urgency: she is inaccessible, her resources reserved for one person. The *na'ul* (locked/bolted) garden requires a key — the beloved's exclusive access.
13: 'Your branches are an orchard of pomegranates with every choice fruit' — the *pardes* (orchard/paradise garden) is a Persian loanword (*pairi-daeza*, walled garden) that entered Hebrew from Akkadian; it appears three times in the OT (Song of Solomon 4:13; Ecclesiastes 2:5; Nehemiah 2:8) and becomes the source of the Greek *paradeisos* (paradise). The beloved's garden is stocked with the most valuable fruits and aromatic plants: pomegranates, henna, nard — all luxury items. The catalogue of spices reflects the international trade network that brought India, Arabia, and the Near East together in the spice trade.
14: Nard (*nerd*), saffron (*karkom*), calamus (*qaneh*), cinnamon (*qinnamon*), frankincense (*levonah*), myrrh (*mor*), and aloes (*ahalot*) — a remarkable concentration of luxury imports. Saffron was from the crocus of Cilicia and Persia; calamus and cinnamon came from India via Arabia; aloes (*ahalot*) were a fragrant wood from southeast Asia (the Indian *aguru* or eaglewood, Aquilaria species). The concentration of international spices in the Song reflects the Solomonic era's trade connections (1 Kings 10:14-15; the spice trade through Arabia).
15: 'A garden fountain, a well of fresh water, streams flowing from Lebanon' — the sealed spring of v12 is now opened, or perhaps the sealed spring is contrasted with the bubbling fountain of her love once the garden is entered. Fresh water (*mayim chayyim*, living/running water) was the most valued commodity in the ancient near east, scarce and precious. The image of streams flowing down from Lebanon — the snow-fed mountain streams that brought life to the lowlands — makes the beloved the source of life-giving abundance.
16: 'Awake, O north wind; come, O south wind; blow through my garden' — the north and south winds in Israel had different characters: the north wind (*tzafon*) brought cold and rain; the south wind (*teiman*) brought warmth and sometimes the dry *sirocco* from the desert. Together they represent all winds — the beloved invites the full force of nature to carry her fragrance to the beloved. The invitation 'let my beloved come to his garden' shifts the possessive: the locked garden of v12 is now 'his garden' — she belongs to him; the invitation completes the transfer. The garden that was locked (v12) is now offered freely.
